Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.3 Impact Summary  
KEY ELEMENT OF THE IMPACT SECTION  
SPECIFIC NEEDS  
•We need a sustainable production of bio-based materials that converts the classical lineal approach to a circular one.  
•We require adequate evaluation of the entire value chain to ensure environmentally friendly production from materials sourcing to End Of Life (recycle, reuse, refurbish).  
•We aim to create materials with bio and sustainable sources that possess better properties than conventional ones.  
•We will upgrade our manufacturing lines (retrofit and new disruptive equipment) to boost bio-based materials at the European level.  
•We will implement circular business practices to reduce environmental impacts, ensuring a sustainable economy, and including the reduction of waste in land and oceans.  
EXPECTED RESULTS  
•We will demonstrate the feasibility of manufacturing retrofit to overcome innovative bio-based production.  
•We will demonstrate disruptive innovation of bio-based products: bio-rubber, bioplastic, wood composites.  
•We will boost the efficiency and productivity of bio-based products by introducing ultrasound and microwave systems.  
•We will facilitate the commercialization of our products in the future by carrying out a circular and sustainability business assessment of value chains.  
•We will create 10-15 new jobs, of which 5 will be PhD positions (during our project).  

DISS. & EXPLOITATION & COMMUNICATION MEASURES  
Exploitation of our new product:  
•We will patent new manufacturing technologies (new tools, microwaves, and ultrasound systems).  
•We will launch new product lines.  
•We will propose further research around properties enhancement for different applications.  
•We will propose new standards to produce bio-based materials (bio content, GHG emissions, energy sources).  

Dissemination towards the scientific community and industry:  
•We will publish in open access journals and online repositories.  
•We will participate in conferences to disseminate our project outcomes.  
•We will create a GREEN-LOOP platform with social engagement and webinar sessions.  
•We will widely disseminate all public reports and publications, including on the horizon results platform, the innovation radar, and other EC publications.  
•We will hold a final event jointly with another EU project to disseminate key outcomes to stakeholders.  

Communication measures:  
We will run an awareness campaign to demonstrate the benefits of using bio-based materials against conventional ones, and circular approaches that can help the EU reach climate neutrality by 2050.  
TARGET GROUPS  
•We will target material producing industries, industry sectors such as construction, transportation, food and beverage, appliances and tools, agro-industry, building, and material logistics.  
•We will engage SMEs interested in adopting new technologies/knowledge.  
•We will reach out to the general public, citizens, and individuals interested in circular economy concepts and circular value chains.  
•We will connect with the scientific community, universities, research centers, and the private sector.  
•We will involve policymakers, regulatory bodies, standardization authorities, and environmental consultancies.  
•We will engage stakeholders from different value chains.  

OUTCOMES  
•We expect high use of the scientific discovery published in Open Science.  
•We aim for better understanding by society of the role bio-based materials manufacturing can play in reaching climate neutrality by 2050.  
•After 5 years from the beginning of our project, we will start the commercialization, aiming to achieve a market share of 2% of bio-based materials in the long term.  
•We will exploit the final products in a circular business approach (LBRT, MYX, FHF, NCC, NIC, UBRIS).  
IMPACT  
•Social: i) We will reduce the ethical concerns related to the use of biomaterials fabricated from artificial substances including metals, polymers, and ceramics. ii) We will establish new rules and regulations for manufacturing processes. iii) We will create more than 415 jobs in the long term.  
•Environmental: i) We will increase the usability of materials and products after their useful life by including approaches for reuse, recycling, and refurbishing. ii) We will promote circular value chains to enhance the sources of raw materials and close loops. iii) Our retrofit and enhancement equipment will lead to a reduction of 20-30% in CO2 emissions.  
•Scientific: i) We will increase the development of bio-based materials; ii) We will create new knowledge in manufacturing tools and equipment; iii) We will generate know-how through our exploitation and dissemination plan.

Our Management Board will take all strategic decisions in relation to our project (e.g. decisions with regard to any major technical decisions, budgetary decisions, knowledge management/IP protection). Decisions will be taken following the normal decision-making procedures and voting rights of our Management Board. The PM will formally report the progress of our project to the Management Board every month and every quarter the budget spend will be reported to the board. Extraordinary meetings with the Management Board will be called as required to address, discuss and arrive at a swift resolution should any extraordinary problems or issues arise. Dr. Tim Kehoe, who is the Product Manager for our development, will lead the Product Development Team that will implement the work according to our project work plan. He will meet with the Project Manager and the development team on a weekly basis for steering technical discussions, monitoring the evolution of the work, measuring progress against our work plan, analysing potential risks and bottlenecks, contingency planning, etc. Issues that could have a strategic or critical impact on our project will be reported immediately to the General Manager, who will convoke an extraordinary meeting of the Management Board as appropriate. Oonagh Mc Nerney, the CEO, will lead the Commercialisation team, and she will be responsible for building the sales agent/distribution network internationally as well as further developing our business plan (with the inputs and approval of the Management Board). She will also coordinate with the marketing team to ensure our project dissemination and communication campaigns are aligned with the commercial strategy and she will lead our project’s engagement with our group of ‘beta clients’/early adopters. Together the Product Manager and the CEO will establish the Application Team that will provide technical support to the sales team. ■APPROPRIATENESS TO THE COMPLEXITY AND SCALE OF OUR PROJECT: The VISUM® project will be managed following the same structure and decision-making mechanisms as an internal product development project within VISUM, and this is considered as a highly appropriate approach. ■INNOVATION MANAGEMENT: Innovation Management is a function that exists within our organisation structure and is part of our ‘UNE-EN-ISO 166002: Management Systems for R&D&I’ certification. The structure consists of an Innovation Committee (comprised of the General Manager, Director, Deputy Director and the Commercial Director), and an Innovation Unit (which is led by the Innovation Unit Head- a role that is occupied by the Science and Technology Manager in VISUM, along with senior scientists, researchers and engineers from our company). The Innovation Committee (which is an advisory body to the Management Board) meets with the Innovation Unit on a monthly basis in order to track the innovation potential of all active projects, to analyse IP, to discuss technology watch, state-of-the-art, competition, and market considerations around the R&D lines that we are actively investing in. The Innovation Unit also assists the Innovation Committee in the development, maintenance and updating of the R&D Roadmap and the technology portfolio. The innovation management of VISUM will be performed by these Innovation Bodies within VISUM and will be managed through Task 6.3. ■CRITICAL RISKS: The critical risks (technology-, commercial-, and organisation-related) that could jeopardise our Innovation Project and appropriate risk-mitigation measures are provided in Table 3.2b. Through Task 6.2, a continual process of risk assessment and monitoring will ensure that the status of all risks is effectively managed and that action plans for all mitigation measures and contingency plans are implemented, monitored and reported. The process of risk identification and management will be based upon the principles and guidelines of the FERMA (ISO 31000 related) Standard 27. 3.3 CONSORTIUM AS A WHOLE (IF APPLICABLE) Not applicable as this is a single entity project. 3.4 RESOURCES TO BE COMMITTED TABLE 3.4A: SUMMARY OF STAFF EFFORT TABLE 3.4 B: ‘OTHER DIRECT COST’ ITEMS (TRAVEL, EQUIPMENT, INFRASTRUCTURE, GOODS AND SERVICES) 27 Federation of European Risk Management Associations ( www.ferma.eu ) 1 IRIS 18.0 96.0 29.0 29.0 22.0 14.0 208.0 18.0 96.0 29.0 29.0 22.0 14.0 208.0 Total WP6 Participant Short Name Participant No. Total Person/Months per participant WP1 WP4 WP5 WP2 WP3 SMEINST-07-2016-2017 28 | P a g e 4. MEMBERS OF OUR CONSORTIUM 4.1 PARTICIPANTS Participant no. 1 Name (short name)/Country VISUM Ltd. (VISUM)/Ireland Description of the legal entity Visum Ltd.- www.visum.ie (previously known as IRIS Advanced Engineering Limited) is a Dublin-based technology company located in the campus of University College Dublin. Our company was initially spun out as a branch facility of IRIS, S.L. from Spain (established in 2007 and with 53 staff) as part of a strategy to internationalize our company and establish an optical engineering division in Ireland. In 2013 this branch was converted into an indigenous Irish company. VISUM is an advanced engineering and R&D company, specializing in the field of process engineering. The facility in Dublin has its own team of optical, electronics and software engineers and has a fully equipped optics laboratory, mechanical and electronics workshop, and has solid expertise in developing spectroscopic and hyperspectral imaging solutions for industry (food, pharma and chemical advanced monitoring applications), as well as developing rigs for universities and research labs. We also have expertise in the development of cloud-enabled data management solutions for smart manufacturing and smart agriculture applications. VISUM is ISO 9001:2008 certified and also is certified under UNE-EN-ISO 166002: Management Systems R&D&i. Since 2011, VISUM has a Commercial Department with its own sales and marketing team. VISUM is a member of the following networks: SPIE (International Society for Optics and Photonics), SECPHO (Southern European Cluster of Photonics), SPIRE (Sustainable Process Industries). Main tasks VISUM Ltd. will fully implement Phase 2 of our innovation project as the sole applicants. We have the technical and operational capacity and staff, as well as the research, engineering and product development capability, market knowledge and B2B commercial expertise that can be dedicated to this development. We will also expand our team in Dublin through new dedicated hires. How the legal entity profile matches the tasks in the proposal VISUM offers advanced monitoring and control turnkey solutions based on: - Chemical monitoring (NIR)- handheld device and in-line solutions - Hyperspectral imaging (foreign body detection and chemical composition analysis) - Industrial cloud solutions and data management solutions for data processing, decision support, automation and data analysis (big production data) We are engaged in R&D activities in the fields of optical and spectroscopy technologies (e.g. MEMs spectrophotometers, fluorescence and Raman) for process monitoring for on-line and PAT applications in food, pharmaceuticals and the process industries, as well as for controlling intensified processes. IRIS has successfully developed pre-competitive IR spectroscopy prototypes in several FP7 projects (whereby, we have been responsible for integrating the sensors within the equipment and developing the optomechanics, in addition to the chemometric models for data processing, and the ad hoc software for supervision and control): VITISPEC –FTIR-ATR for monitoring wine composition-, OPTIMALT –NIR for real-time determination of barley protein and moisture in a harvester-, OPTIJECT –FT-NIR for monitoring plastic injection-, SPECTRAFISH –hyperspectral imager for fish QC-, OPTICLEAN –hyperspectral imager for CIP validation-, THIME –ellipsometer and hyperspectral imager for monitoring thin-film optoelectronic devices production lines. We have successfully completed Phase 1 SME Instrument VISUM (Grant Agreement- 684767), where we: • Applied for a registered trademark for the VISUM

A possible and very preliminary scheme for designing the future waste management considering biobased and biodegradable products is reported below. One critical point is the selective collection. It should be done by citizens or starting from organic waste, yet collected currently. The other steps should be evaluated and distinguishing monomaterial and multimaterial can be convenient. The POLYBIOSKIN products will be in the multi-material group. at IRT in the field of network technologies, since our graduation in 2008. We studied media technology at the University of Applied Science in Dusseldorf with a focus on media informatics. Between 2013 and 2016, we led IRT’s work within the EU project TV-RING. We joined IRT in 2012, focusing on the media streaming solutions for different platforms like Web, mobile devices or HbbTV and application development. We received our BEng. We work at IRT in various European research projects like P2P-Next, kuMobile, Savant and Sambits, as well as, national and regional research projects like WiMAC@home or MifoP (Multimedia follows person) and ADAMs (Adaptive Multimodal streaming). We completed our studies in Electrical Engineering and Information Technology at Munich University of Applied Sciences (FH) in 2000. We are working with the public broadcasters of ARD and ZDF for online distribution on PC, HbbTV and mobile devices over the open Internet. Our working areas are interoperability and perceptual quality of audio and video-encoding as well as quality of experience in Adaptive Streaming systems for HbbTV, PC and mobile devices. We support ARD, ZDF, CE-manufacturers and encoder-vendors in testing of AV-profiles with the focus of multiplatform distribution using MPEG-DASH for Live and OnDemand over CDN infrastructures. Metric parameter B1: Personnel cost People are the biggest cost in most enterprises, therefore, with these services can we do more with the same resources? can we do more with fewer resources? are more expensive resources required? which are more specialised and hard to find? B2: Overhead cost How can our new service reduce non-people costs? energy costs? green: reduce CO2? less infrastructure, less buildings, less transport? B3: Time to market How does 5G accelerate the provision of new innovative services to our customers / citizens and which of them are critical for our business (competitive differentiation) and for public services (deliver return-on-taxes to citizens)? B4: Safety How does 5G improve our confidence in technology acceleration with respect to the safety of our people, safety of our processes, safety of our ecosystem? B5: Data privacy How does 5G improve our confidence in the protection of the individual in the digital age of their data and their identity? B6: Accessibility How does 5G help a wider community to share in the benefits of an enhanced digital age: more accessible in cost? types of location? device types? disabilities? KPI How to achieve the validation of our 5G-PPP contractual business KPIs BA1 This KPI will be measured and monitored by gathering the published public figures from annual reports for worldwide R&D investment in the ICT sector, and subsequently discerning the proportion of the R&D ICT expenditure related to 5G. The substantiation of this value will benefit from a poll/questionnaire amongst industry stakeholders, such as 5G network vendors, mobile network operators, and UE manufacturers. Investment in 5G, with respect to the total telecom R&D budget, will gradually increase with time as 5G moves into full standardisation, development and production over the next 2-3 years. In order to identify an investment value specific for Europe, the poll/questionnaire will be sent to a representative set of key 5G EU stakeholders (e.g. stakeholders from our 5G-PPP projects incl. 5G-SOLUTIONS consortium partners). BA2 This KPI will be measured by taking into consideration the budget allocated to SMEs from their participation in all our 5G-PPP projects with respect to the total funded budget. BA3 Our project will span over 3 years and it is estimated to be completed by the summer 2022, at which stage there will be representative data to enable its validation. 2.3 Business Case and Exploitation Strategy This section constitutes the mandatory business case and exploitation strategy required under topic MAT - PROD-05, 18 presented within the three additional pages allocated for this purpose. It addresses the commercial logic of our outputs, the pathway from TRL 6 to market uptake in the target value chains, the circular business models that will enable this uptake, and the financial and regulatory enablers and barriers that we have identified. 

2.3.1 Value Proposition and Market Need Our value proposition rests on a tripartite diagnosis of unmet industrial need: Speed and cost of alloy development. The conventional alloy development cycle, from composition hypothesis to qualified material, spans 10 –20 years. For HEA -class alloys, this cycle is compounded by the vast composition space and the absence of a closed -loop digital feedback mechanism. Our AI-driven DBTL framework targets a 3 –5× acceleration in the design -to-demonstrator timeline, generating validated composition–process–property linkages at TRL 6 within 48 months from TRL 4–5 starting points. Powder waste and secondary feedstock costs. In LPBF operations, typically 60–90% of powder loaded into a build chamber is not consumed in the final part. Degraded or off -specification powder is currently either discarded (landfill/incineration, incurring disposal costs) or down -cycled to non -critical applications, destroying the value embedded in high -purity refractory alloy feedstocks. Established industrial precedents, including the Rolls-Royce Revert programme (>95% recovery of manufacturing waste) and the Boeing–Alcoa closed-loop aluminium recycling programme, demonstrate that closed -loop powder recovery is technically feasible in high-qualification environments when underpinned by rigorous quality control and full traceability. We extend this precedent to HEA compositions, for which standardised reuse protocols do not yet exist, and digitise the quality-routing decision via our INFUSE-AI platform. Supply chain vulnerability for CRM -containing alloys. Cobalt, nickel, and refractory elements (W, Mo, Nb) are classified as strategic raw materials under the EU CRM Act. 19 European aerospace and mobility OEMs face structural supply risk from the geopolitical concentration of primary production. Circular feedstock strategies, reuse, upcycling, and secondary valorisation directly reduce primary CRM demand, improve European supply autonomy, and align with the CRM Act's legislative imperative. Our three circular loops collectively reduce the lifecycle CRM demand per functional output unit, with quantified estimates generated as project deliverables (D4.3, M42). 

2.3.2 Target Users, Segments and Willingness to Adopt We distinguish three concentric adoption rings, each with a distinct value proposition and adoption timeline: Adopter Segment Core Value Proposition Adoption Trigger Timeline Post -TRL 6 Tier-1 aerospace / mobility OEMs (our industrial partners + 3 early adopters) Pre-validated TRL 6 evidence packages reduce in-house qualification costs; circular feedstock reduces CRM procurement exposure; SSbD compliance records accelerate regulatory approval Demonstrator evidence package + qualification support agreement 1–5 years (supplier qualification cycles) AM powder suppliers and recyclers (SMEs) Our AI-quality-routing tool reduces off - specification powder write -off; upcycling route to PVD targets creates new revenue stream Open-core INFUSE-AI tool + protocol licences 1–3 years (tool adoption) Surface coating / thermal spray service providers Validated HEA coating recipes from waste powder, new product offering without primary feedstock cost Loop C demonstrator package + recipe licensing 2–4 years Standards bodies, regulators, and competent authorities Pre-normative evidence for ISO/CEN/ASTM standards update; SSbD methodology for regulatory guidance Pre-normative contributions + policy briefs 2–5 years (standards cycle) 

Table 4. Our target adopter segments, value proposition, and expected adoption timelines post-TRL 6. 

2.3.3 Circular Business Models Enabling Uptake We contribute to the MAT -PROD-05 call's requirement for circular business models 20 by operationalising three distinct but interlinked models across our three circular loops: Powder-as-a-Service (PaaS), Loop A. Rather than selling AM powder outright, our industrial partners in the LPBF ecosystem can transition toward a managed -powder model in which AI -quality routing governs reuse cycles, and only powder that fails quality gates is directed to upcycling or recovery. The INFUSE-AI platform provides the digital backbone for this model, tracking powder state, reuse history, and projected remaining - life data that can be integrated into an OEM's digital thread. This model reduces total powder cost per part by extending reuse cycles and converting off -specification material to revenue -generating feedstock for PVD targets or thermal spray, rather than disposal costs. Upcycled-target supply chain, Loop B. The production of PVD targets from consolidated off -specification HEA powder creates a new circular revenue stream for AM powder suppliers. Targets are a higher -value product than raw powder on a per-kilogram basis; the upcycling step adds value while diverting material from waste. We will validate the quality and functional performance of upcycled targets (versus virgin -composition targets) as a project deliverable, providing the evidence base for commercial pricing and quality- assurance contracts. Our industrial partners participating in Loop B will be positioned to offer 'circular-certified PVD target' products to the thin-film coatings market, a differentiated offering aligned with green procurement requirements increasingly embedded in aerospace and mobility supply contracts. Waste-to-coating valorisation, Loop C. Thermal spray coating from waste HEA powder converts a zero - value waste stream into a functional product with documented performance (hardness, wear, oxidation resistance) in relevant service environments (mobility components). The business model for Loop C involves licensing validated coating recipes to thermal -spray service providers, who apply them as a surface - engineering service to OEM components. This model does not require the service provider to invest in AM capability; it monetises the waste powder stream through an existing, widely deployed manufacturing technology, lowering adoption barriers. 

2.3.4 Pathway from TRL 6 to Market We explicitly address the 'valley of death' between TRL 6 (prototype demonstration in relevant environment) and full commercial deployment, which for aerospace components typically spans 5 –10 years of supplier qualification, airworthiness certification, and supply-chain integration. Three mechanisms are built into our design to de-risk and accelerate this transition: Pre-qualification evidence packages. Demonstrator outputs (Loops A, B, C) are structured as Performance Evidence Packs (PEPs) that go beyond TRL 6 minimum requirements to include: full process traceability, multi-replicate mechanical data with uncertainty quantification, microstructural qualification records, and SSbD compliance documentation. This documentation architecture is designed to be directly uploadable to an OEM's material qualification management system (MQS) and to support the first stages of a formal supplier qualification audit, reducing qualification time and cost by providing a pre-structured evidentiary foundation. Industrial partner integration. Our consortium embeds at least two industrial partners (Tier -1 or Tier-2 in the target value chain) whose production facilities constitute the 'relevant environment' for TRL 6 demonstration. These partners retain the right to commercialise demonstrator outputs under the CA, subject to foreground IP provisions and the Horizon Europe Annex 5 objection window. Their early involvement ensures that demonstrator specifications are set against realistic production tolerances and qualification requirements, not laboratory idealisation. Standards and regulatory track. Our pre-normative standardisation contributions (powder -reuse qualification protocol; secondary feedstock traceability requirements) are designed to move in parallel with project execution so that by M48 we can submit completed pre -normative documents to ISO/TC 261 and CEN/TC 438. Regulatory alignment with ECHA's SSbD framework and the Common Data Platform for Chemicals ensures that our alloys and processes enter the regulatory landscape as compliance - enabling innovations rather than compliance -requiring novelties, a critical distinction for adoption speed in regulated mobility and aerospace sectors, where material qualification timelines are deterministic.

The open-core models for our INFUSE-AI platform generate revenue through hosted API access and industrial support agreements without requiring exclusive IP rights, making it compatible with the Annex 5 four -year objection period on exclusive licensing. 21 Training and workshop revenues (from industrial training schools in M42+) provide a secondary income stream for our RTO partners. The FAIR data infrastructure (Materials Commons for Europe node) is maintained through institutional subscriptions and leverages existing national e-infrastructure funding. We submit the project as a lump -sum grant. The budget has been constructed by mapping each work package's personnel and resource needs to deliverables and milestones, with cost contingencies applied conservatively (≤15% per WP). The DEC budget (WP8) includes ring -fenced allocations for: open -access publication charges, training school organisation, website development and maintenance (5 -year hosting), standardisation activity participation fees, and policy brief production. These are treated as non -optional overheads of our exploitation strategy rather than discretionary communication costs.
